mnemonics.title: Linked List Node Parts
mnemonics.content: DATA + NEXT = D A T A N E X T (Data Next) - Remember: DATA comes FIRST, NEXT points to what's NEXT

mnemonics.title: Linked List Advantages
mnemonics.content: DYNAMIC - D=Dynamic size, Y=Yes (efficient insertions), N=No wasted memory, A=Always available, M=Memory efficient, I=Insertions easy, C=Contiguous not required, S=Scalable

mnemonics.title: Singly vs Doubly
mnemonics.content: SINGLY = ONE WAY STREET (can only go forward), DOUBLY = TWO WAY STREET (can go both directions)

mnemonics.title: Cycle Detection
mnemonics.content: FLOYD = FAST SLOW - Fast moves 2, Slow moves 1, if they meet there's a cycle

acronyms.usage: Abbreviation used in code comments and technical discussions
Starting point for all linked list operations
Basic building block of a linked list
Indicates the last node in a singly linked list

visualTips.visualization: Think of a TRAIN with each car (node) having PASSENGERS (data) and a COUPLER (next pointer) connecting to the next car. The last car has no coupler (NULL).
Imagine adding a new train car at the FRONT: connect its coupler to the current front car, then make it the new front car.
Think of following a treasure map where each instruction leads to the next location. Start at HEAD and follow NEXT pointers until you reach the end (NULL).
Picture a DOUBLED-ENDED train with couplers on BOTH ends of each car - one for the car in front, one for the car behind.

analogies.analogy: Arrays are like RESERVED SEATS in a theater (fixed positions, can directly go to seat 15). Linked lists are like a SCAVENGER HUNT (must follow clues from start to reach destination).
Pointers are like STREET ADDRESSES written on letters - they tell you where to go next but aren't the actual destination.
Linked list is like HOTEL ROOMS being booked as guests arrive (rooms allocated on-demand), while arrays are like pre-assigned hotel floors (entire floor reserved upfront).
Removing a node is like REMOVING A LINK from a chain - you connect the links that were on either side of the removed link.
